questions.prompt: Grammar — Past perfect: "By the time the ambulance arrived, the doctors ___ already started treatment."
questions.explanation: "By the time" signals that one past action was completed before another, so we use the past perfect: the doctors had already started. "have" is present perfect (wrong tense), "were" would begin a continuous form (needs -ing), and "did" is the past simple auxiliary (no participle follows it).

questions.prompt: Grammar — Passive voice: "This road ___ every winter because of heavy snowfall."
questions.explanation: A habitual, recurring event using a passive (the road is the object of closing) takes is + past participle: is closed. "closes" is active — it implies the road acts. "was closing" is a past continuous (scene-setting, not habitual), and "had closed" is the past perfect (wrong time frame here).

questions.prompt: Grammar — Reported speech: "Sara said, 'I will call you tonight.' " What did Sara say?

options.text: Sara said she would call me that night.
Sara said she will call me tonight.
Sara said she called me that night.
Sara said I would call her tonight.

questions.explanation: Reported speech back-shifts will → would and tonight → that night, and changes the pronoun you → me: she would call me that night. (b) keeps will and tonight — no back-shift. (c) uses the past simple instead of would. (d) swaps the subject and object (I would call her) — wrong perspective.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Third conditional: "If they ___ earlier, they wouldn't have missed the train."
questions.explanation: The third conditional expresses an impossible past: if-clause = had + participle, result = would have + participle. "If they had left earlier" is correct. "would leave" wrongly puts a modal in the if-clause, "left" is the second conditional (imaginary present/future), and "have left" is the present perfect (wrong auxiliary for a past impossible).

questions.prompt: Usage — Gerund or infinitive: "She always avoids ___ in crowded places."
questions.explanation: avoid is always followed by a gerund (-ing), never an infinitive: She avoids speaking. "to speak" (infinitive) is wrong after avoid — compare with want/decide which take the infinitive. "speak" is the bare infinitive (used only after modals and make/let/help), and "spoke" is the past simple.
